title: Devonshire cream scones
subtitle: Classic cream tea scones, plain or fruit
excerpt: Ten light, well-risen scones for a cream tea: a simple dough of flour, butter, and milk, plain or with a handful of sultanas. Serve split with clotted cream and raspberry jam; in Devon the cream goes on before the jam.
sourceNotes: Cream scones for a cream tea are documented in West Country British cookery from at least the late nineteenth century. Recipes appear in pre-1928 Devon and Cornwall household cookery manuscripts and in domestic science publications of the period; the cream tea format with clotted cream and jam is attested in pre-1928 travel accounts of Devon and in early-twentieth-century British newspaper columns available through the British Newspaper Archive. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), gives a plain scone recipe of comparable form.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat in a 180°C oven for 8 minutes from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Scones are best on the day. If making ahead, underbake by 2 minutes and finish in the oven on the day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 230°C. High heat is essential for the rise. Scones should be fully baked in 12 to 14 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: The question of whether clotted cream or jam goes on a cream tea scone first is a real regional dispute in the South West of England. In Devon, the cream goes on first (as a base), then jam on top. In Cornwall, the jam goes on first, then cream. Neither is objectively correct. The scone itself should be split horizontally with a knife rather than pulled apart, to give a flat surface for the toppings.

content.text: Devonshire cream scones are a plain or fruit-studded scone served with clotted cream and jam. The
is a matter of county loyalty; the scone itself is the same either way. Ten scones, ready in 25 minutes. Light hands and a hot oven are the only technique.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan (230°C conventional). Line a baking tray. Sift the flour, baking powder, salt, and sugar into a large bowl. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Stir in the sultanas if using. Beat the egg with the milk, then pour into the flour mixture and stir with a knife until the dough just comes together; it will look rough and sticky. Do not stir further.

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and pat gently to 2.5 cm thick. Cut with a 6 cm round cutter, pressing straight down. Place on the lined tray; the scones can touch. Brush the tops with a little of the remaining egg-milk mixture. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until risen and deep golden. Eat warm, split, and topped with clotted cream and raspberry jam.
